title: 1981-04-16_Pat_145.txt
key_claims: Dick formally abandons his Faustian striving and Gnostic rebellion ("the taste of the apple justifies the Fall") in favor of Orthodox submission to God's will. He recognizes that God's law maintains cosmic order and is the highest good.
He achieves peace through resignation: "His will is my peace." God blocked his affair and his destructive strivings because He acts as both adversary (Heimarmene) and advocate.

By perceiving reality's extension into infinity (Spinoza's third attribute), one sees Spinoza's God (*Deus sive Natura*). In 1974, Dick saw particulars extended into the infinite, transforming them into their own eternal archetypes.
Malebranche's Occasionalism is proven correct: Dick could not will himself to pursue Sandra because God, acting within him, simply decreed against it.
recurring_concepts: **Felix Culpa**: The "fortunate fall." The realization that the tragedy and suffering caused by God's strict laws ultimately give birth to a greater victory (Christ/Ecstasy).
reading_excerpt: Dick formally abandons his Faustian striving and Gnostic rebellion ("the taste of the apple justifies the Fall") in favor of Orthodox submission to God's will. He recognizes that God's law maintains cosmic order and is the highest good.
